From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

British Labour politician (born 1961)

**Grahame Mark Morris**[[1]](#cite_note-Crown_Office-1) (born 13 March 1961)[[2]](#cite_note-2) is a [British Labour Party](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)") politician who has been the [Member of Parliament](/wiki/Member_of_Parliament_(United_Kingdom) "Member of Parliament (United Kingdom)") (MP) for [Easington](/wiki/Easington_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Easington (UK Parliament constituency)") since [2010](/wiki/2010_United_Kingdom_general_election "2010 United Kingdom general election").

Morris briefly served on the [Opposition frontbench of Jeremy Corbyn](/wiki/Opposition_frontbench_of_Jeremy_Corbyn "Opposition frontbench of Jeremy Corbyn") in 2016, and now remains in the [House of Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_the_United_Kingdom "House of Commons of the United Kingdom") as a [backbencher](/wiki/Backbencher "Backbencher").

## Early life and career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grahame_Morris&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life and career")]

Grahame Morris was born on 13 March 1961, and was educated at Howletch Comprehensive School (now [East Durham College](/wiki/East_Durham_College "East Durham College")). He first worked as a medical laboratory scientific officer in hospitals in [Sunderland](/wiki/Sunderland "Sunderland"). Morris has been involved with politics since 1987 when he became a District Councillor for Easington. He worked as a researcher for his predecessor MP for Easington [John Cummings](/wiki/John_Cummings_(politician) "John Cummings (politician)") since 1997, and was also Secretary of Easington Labour Party.

## Parliamentary career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grahame_Morris&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Parliamentary career")]

At the [2010 general election](/wiki/2010_United_Kingdom_general_election "2010 United Kingdom general election"), Morris was elected to Parliament as MP for [Easington](/wiki/Easington_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Easington (UK Parliament constituency)") with 58.9% of the vote and a majority of 14,982.[[3]](#cite_note-electoralcalculus2010-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5) He was one of a handful of Labour MPs newly elected in 2010 considered to be on the left of the party politically.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

Morris chairs the [Labour Friends of Palestine and the Middle East](/wiki/Labour_Friends_of_Palestine_and_the_Middle_East "Labour Friends of Palestine and the Middle East").[[7]](#cite_note-Dysch,-7) In October 2014, he presented the motion in Parliament calling on the government to formally recognise [Palestinian statehood](/wiki/Palestinian_statehood "Palestinian statehood").[[8]](#cite_note-8)

Morris was one of 16 signatories of an open letter to [Ed Miliband](/wiki/Ed_Miliband "Ed Miliband") in January 2015 calling on the party to commit to oppose further austerity, take rail franchises back into public ownership and strengthen collective bargaining arrangements.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

At the [2015 general election](/wiki/2015_United_Kingdom_general_election "2015 United Kingdom general election"), Morris was re-elected as MP for Easington with an increased vote share of 61% and a decreased majority of 14,641.[[10]](#cite_note-electoralcalculus2015-10)

Morris was one of 36 Labour MPs to nominate [Jeremy Corbyn](/wiki/Jeremy_Corbyn "Jeremy Corbyn") as a candidate in the [Labour leadership election of 2015](/wiki/2015_Labour_Party_leadership_election_(UK) "2015 Labour Party leadership election (UK)"), after the 2015 general election.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

On 27 June 2016, Morris was appointed to the [Shadow Cabinet](/wiki/Official_Opposition_Shadow_Cabinet_(United_Kingdom) "Official Opposition Shadow Cabinet (United Kingdom)") as Shadow Secretary of State for Communities and Local Government and Shadow Minister for the Constitutional Convention.[[12]](#cite_note-sunderlandecho-20160628-12)

Morris was again re-elected at the snap [2017 general election](/wiki/2017_United_Kingdom_general_election "2017 United Kingdom general election"), with an increased vote share of 63.7% and an increased majority of 14,892.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

On 22 April 2019, Morris retweeted a video he had received, supposedly about Palestinian children, and captioned it as Israeli soldiers "caught on camera beating up Palestinian children for the fun of it". However, the video was actually of Guatemalan, not Israeli, soldiers.[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15) Morris later apologised and said it was an "honest mistake".[[16]](#cite_note-16) He referenced the dangers of fake news and said he would check sources with more care in future.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

At the [2019 general election](/wiki/2019_United_Kingdom_general_election "2019 United Kingdom general election"), Morris was again re-elected, with a decreased vote share of 45.5% and a decreased majority of 6,581.[[18]](#cite_note-18) He was again re-elected at the [2024 general election](/wiki/2024_United_Kingdom_general_election "2024 United Kingdom general election"), with an increased vote share of 48.9% and a decreased majority of 6,542.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

## Personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grahame_Morris&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Personal life")]

Morris lives in [Seaham](/wiki/Seaham "Seaham"), [County Durham](/wiki/County_Durham "County Durham").[[20]](#cite_note-Profile-20) He is a prominent supporter of [Sunderland A.F.C.](/wiki/Sunderland_A.F.C. "Sunderland A.F.C.")[[21]](#cite_note-21)

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grahame_Morris&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Notes")]
